name: Gaudapada (Samkhya commentator)
dates_notes: The author of the Sāṃkhya-Kārikā-Bhāṣya is conventionally distinguished from the Vedānta Gauḍapāda of the Māṇḍūkya-Kārikā by Larson (1979, Classical Sāṃkhya, p. 152) and Frauwallner (Geschichte I, pp. 226–229), on the grounds that the Sāṃkhya-bhāṣya's prose style and doctrinal commitments are incompatible with ajātivāda. King (1995) and a minority current argue for identification; standard scholarly convention treats them as separate. The bhāṣya is dated by the terminus ante quem of Paramārtha's Chinese translation (c. 557–569 CE), which is either of this work or of a closely related commentary on the Kārikā.
core_thesis: Gauḍapāda's *Sāṃkhya-Kārikā-Bhāṣya* fixes the standard exegetical reading of Īśvarakṛṣṇa's seventy verses and is the earliest extant prose commentary on the *Kārikā*. The interpretive choices made here propagate through the entire later commentarial stream. The *bhāṣya* takes the *Kārikā*'s opening *duḥkha-traya* analysis (*Bhāṣya* on *Sāṃkhya-Kārikā* 1) as the school's motivating problem, develops *satkārya-vāda* with the standard five-fold inferential support (*asad-akaraṇa*, *upādāna-grahaṇa*, *sarva-sambhava-abhāva*, *śaktasya śakya-karaṇa*, *kāraṇa-bhāva* — *Bhāṣya* on *Sāṃkhya-Kārikā* 9), and articulates the *vyakta-avyakta-sādharmya* inference for *prakṛti* in the form that becomes canonical (*Bhāṣya* on *Sāṃkhya-Kārikā* 14–16). The *guṇa*-analysis is given in its mature form (*Bhāṣya* on *Sāṃkhya-Kārikā* 12–13): *sattva*, *rajas*, *tamas* are not merely qualities adhering to *prakṛti* but are themselves *prakṛti*'s constitutive substance in disequilibrium. *Puruṣa*-plurality is defended on three converging grounds — birth-and-death asymmetry, simultaneous-engagement asymmetry, *guṇa*-disposition asymmetry — taken from *Sāṃkhya-Kārikā* 18 (with *Bhāṣya* ad loc.). The *bhāṣya*'s historical significance is its accessibility: where the *Yuktidīpikā* is technical and Vāchaspati's later *Tattva-Kaumudī* is dialectically loaded, Gauḍapāda gives a clean expository reading that becomes the entry-text into Sāṃkhya for medieval Indian readers and for the first European Sanskritists (Colebrooke 1827; Wilson 1837 published the *Kārikā* with this *bhāṣya* as the standard accompaniment).

engaged_works.title: Samkhya-Karika-Bhashya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composition fixed by terminus ante quem of Paramārtha's mid-sixth-century Chinese translation of a closely related Sāṃkhya commentary.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely ascribed within the Sāṃkhya commentarial tradition to the Sāṃkhya Gauḍapāda; the identification with the Vedānta Gauḍapāda of the Māṇḍūkya-Kārikā is rejected by mainstream scholarship (Larson 1979; Frauwallner 1953) on grounds of incompatible doctrinal commitments.

ascription_evidence.description: Author-identity debate: standard convention (followed here) treats the Sāṃkhya Gauḍapāda as distinct from the Vedānta Gauḍapāda.

engaged_works.summary: Verse-by-verse prose commentary on the *Sāṃkhya-Kārikā*. The text is brief and exegetically transparent: each *kārikā* is glossed phrase-by-phrase, the technical terms are defined in their first occurrence, and the inferential structure is made explicit where the verse compresses it. Three sections receive especially careful treatment. First, the *Kārikā*'s opening on the *duḥkha-traya* and the inadequacy of ordinary remedies (*Bhāṣya* on *Sāṃkhya-Kārikā* 1–2), which Gauḍapāda turns into a structured argument for the necessity of *viveka-jñāna*. Second, *Kārikā* 9–11 on *satkārya-vāda* (*Bhāṣya* on *Sāṃkhya-Kārikā* 9), where the *bhāṣya* lays out the five canonical proofs in the form that becomes standard. Third, the *Kārikā*'s closing verses on *bhāva*-causation and the *liṅga-śarīra* (*Bhāṣya* on *Sāṃkhya-Kārikā* 40–55), where the *bhāṣya*'s expository clarity rescues a notoriously compressed passage. The *bhāṣya*'s work is intra-traditional clarification rather than polemic, and the Sāṃkhya analysis is offered as complete in its own right.
